covid_19.explain_yes_answers_here_covid_.answer:
Ethan tested positive for COVID-19 in late March 2021 after a known exposure on his club soccer team. He developed mild to moderate symptoms, including low-grade fever (up to 100.8°F), dry cough, temporary loss of taste and smell, and fatigue. His symptoms started on 2021-03-24, and he received a positive PCR test result on 2021-03-26 at the Riverside Community Health Clinic in Austin, TX.

He quarantined at home in his own bedroom, with his parents bringing him meals and limiting contact. No one in the household required hospitalization, and Ethan never had any chest pain, shortness of breath at rest, or difficulty breathing that would have required urgent care or emergency room evaluation. His oxygen saturation at home (checked with a fingertip pulse oximeter that his dad owns) remained between 97–99% throughout the illness. The most bothersome symptoms for him were fatigue and the loss of taste and smell, which gradually improved over about 2–3 weeks. By mid-April 2021, he had fully returned to his normal energy level and appetite, and by early May 2021 his sense of taste and smell had returned to baseline.

Although he did not need any special heart or lung tests and was not referred to a cardiologist, his pediatrician, Dr. Laura Chen at Barton Creek Pediatrics, did a telehealth follow-up visit on 2021-04-05. During that visit, she reviewed his symptoms in detail and specifically asked about chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, or fainting during or after COVID-19. Ethan denied all of these. Because his illness was mild and he had already begun to feel significantly better, she advised a gradual return to sports over a 7–10 day period once he was symptom-free, starting with light jogging and non-contact drills before full scrimmage play.

Ethan followed this guidance closely. He restarted light conditioning (easy jogging and stretching) about two weeks after his positive test, then progressed to more intense practice over the following week. By early May 2021 he was back to full participation in both school PE and club soccer without any lingering symptoms. Since then, he has continued to participate in year-round soccer, including practices 3–4 times per week and weekend games or tournaments, with no issues such as unusual shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, or decreased exercise tolerance.

He has had multiple COVID-19 tests overall: the initial positive PCR test in March 2021 and two later PCR tests for screening purposes before soccer tournaments in late 2021 and early 2022, both of which were negative. He has not had any documented COVID-19 infections since March 2021.

Regarding vaccination, Ethan received the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine series once it became available to his age group. He got his first dose on 2021-06-18 and his second dose on 2021-07-09 at the Austin Community Vaccination Center (run through Travis County Health). He experienced only mild side effects: some soreness at the injection site and mild fatigue and headache for about 24 hours after each dose, but no fever, chest pain, or breathing issues. He then received a booster dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech vaccine on 2022-01-21, also at the same community vaccination clinic. After the booster, he again had mild arm soreness and felt a bit tired the following day but did not miss any practices or school.

In the three months prior to filling out this form, Ethan has not had any known direct exposure to a confirmed COVID-19 case. His school, Westview Middle School, has continued masking and symptom screening policies during surges, and his soccer club requests that players with any respiratory symptoms get tested before returning to practice. Ethan has consistently tested negative on rapid antigen tests when screened for minor cold-like symptoms during allergy season.

Overall, Ethan has fully recovered from his prior COVID-19 infection, has completed his primary vaccination series plus a booster, and has been participating in full, high-intensity sports activities without any limitations or concerning symptoms for well over a year.
